upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows an extremely stable atmosphere with a near-isothermal layer from the surface to 850mb — the lapse rate of only 0.40°C/km (vs 9.8 dry adiabatic) indicates a strong low-level inversion, likely capping thermals well below 1500ft AGL. Winds at 850mb are 21 kts from 005° (nearly northerly), increasing to 21 kts at 700mb from 360°, indicating significant wind shear between the surface and mid-levels. The deep dew point spread (26-28°C) at all upper levels confirms a very dry free atmosphere above the marine boundary layer, with no convective instability (CAPE ~0).
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. METARs unavailable (data aborted) — operating without ground-truth surface obs. Buoy data shows 13.6 kts from 320° at Half Moon Bay and 9.7 kts from 330° at SF Bar, confirming a northwesterly gradient offshore with SST of 12.3–12.5°C indicating a cold, potentially persistent marine boundary layer.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland thermal site, NE-facing launch, valley breeze driven
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin 1750 is primarily a thermal site driven by valley heating. It needs light-to-moderate NW or W winds aloft with a developing thermal cycle; the NE-facing launch works best with a NE morning drainage flow transitioning to valley/sea breeze. Upper winds out of the W-NW help pilots stay up on the ridge.
sites.todaySetup: The deciding factor today is persistent and borderline-strong NE surface winds all day — HRRR shows 13–15 kts from 044–048° at the 10m level throughout the morning and into early afternoon, with 80m winds even stronger at 19–25 kts early on. Tomorrow.io adds gusts of 25–29 kts in the afternoon (1500–1600 local), confirming punchy, mechanical conditions. The sounding's strong low-level inversion (0.40°C/km lapse rate) severely caps thermal development, with the boundary layer only reaching 210–475m AGL through the day — thermals will be weak, broken, and capped well below usable altitude. The combination of too-strong and gusty winds from an unfavorable NE direction, plus a capped thermal environment, makes this site unworkable today. Wind shear between the surface (NE at 13 kts) and 80m level (NE at 20+ kts) adds rotor and turbulence risk on launch and in the LZ.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: HRRR shows 13–15 kts from 044° with 80m winds 19–25 kts — far too strong and sheared for a safe morning session. BL depth only 210–340m, thermals non-existent.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds ease slightly to 13–15 kts at surface but remain NE; gusts per Tomorrow.io reach 25 kts. BL climbing to 375–435m but inversion still severely limits thermal height. Not viable.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Surface winds drop to 11–13 kts from NE/N by 1600–1700, but Tomorrow.io shows gusts still 20–24 kts. BL peaks at 475m — marginal thermal ceiling at best. Shear and gustiness persist through evening.

verdict.hazards: NE winds too strong and gusty all day; strong wind shear 10m vs 80m; thermal ceiling capped under 500m by strong inversion; afternoon gusts 25–29 kts

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training hill launch, valley floor, NE-facing
sites.howItWorks: The 600ft launch is a beginner/training hill that needs light, smooth, on-cycle valley winds — ideally 8–12 kts from the NW or W for foot-launchable conditions. It is far more sensitive to strong or gusty winds than the upper launch given its lower elevation and closer proximity to terrain turbulence.
sites.todaySetup: Conditions at the 600ft launch are worse than at the upper launch today. Early morning HRRR shows an alarming 22 kts from 034° at the 10m level with 80m winds at 32 kts — far beyond any safe operating limit for this training hill. Tomorrow.io adds gusts of 25–30 kts through the afternoon. These are training-hill-ending numbers even for experienced pilots. The strong inversion caps any thermal benefit, and the mechanical turbulence generated by 20+ kt surface flow over the lower terrain makes this site hazardous all day. No window exists.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 22 kts from NE at surface, 28–32 kts at 80m — dangerous, well beyond limits. Hard NO.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds ease to 16–17 kts but Tomorrow.io gusts still 25 kts from NE. Still not safe for a 600ft training launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Surface winds moderate to 13–14 kts by 1600 but gusts remain 22–27 kts. Lower terrain increases rotor/mechanical turbulence risk. Remains NO FLY.

verdict.hazards: Extreme gusts for a training hill; 22 kts surface wind at 0800; rotor/mechanical turbulence; inversion caps thermals under 500m
verdict.skillLevel: P4+ locals only — even then not recommended

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal ridge, W/NW-facing, sea breeze ridge lift site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a classic coastal ridge soaring site powered by the Pacific sea breeze. It needs 12–20 kts from the W or NW for reliable ridge lift. Too light and it's sinkfest; too strong (>22 kts) and it becomes turbulent. The marine layer timing and offshore wind strength drive everything here.
sites.todaySetup: The fundamental problem at Mussel Rock today is that the wind is too light and from the wrong direction for ridge lift. HRRR shows surface winds only 3–6 kts throughout the day, cycling from ESE in the morning to NNW in the afternoon — well below the 12+ kts needed for sustained ridge soaring. Tomorrow.io corroborates with 3–5 kts and gusts only reaching 7 kts max, confirming calm coastal conditions. The offshore buoys at Half Moon Bay (13.6 kts, 320°) and SF Bar (9.7 kts, 330°) indicate a northwesterly gradient exists offshore, but this is clearly not penetrating the coastal zone effectively today. With SST at 12.3–12.5°C and a 12-second swell period at Half Moon Bay, a shallow marine layer is likely stabilizing the coastal air. There is simply not enough wind energy at the ridge face for soarable ridge lift at any point today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 3–4 kts from ESE/E — directly offshore and far too light. No ridge lift possible; thermal potential also nil with inversion in place.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds rotate toward NNW by 1400, reaching 4–5 kts. Still well below the 12+ kts threshold for ridge soaring. Thermal boundary layer reaches 530–725m but too light for sustained flight.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNW flow at 6–7 kts by 1600–1700 is the best of the day but still marginal to light. 80m winds at 8–9 kts (HRRR) suggest a slight improvement aloft but surface remains insufficient for soaring.

verdict.hazards: Winds far too light for ridge lift all day; ESE morning flow (offshore) unfavorable; cold SST stabilizing coastal boundary layer

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge/thermal site, W-facing, sea breeze + thermal hybrid
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak works on a combination of thermal lift and sea breeze-driven ridge lift. It needs W to NW winds of 10–18 kts with a developing thermal cycle. The NE winds and morning drainage flow can make the site work early but too much NE creates turbulent, unfavorable conditions on the W-facing launch.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak shares the same NE wind problem as Ed Levin today. HRRR shows persistent 13–15 kts from 044–048° all morning and into afternoon, with 80m winds 15–25 kts — the wind direction is perpendicular to and partially cross-behind the W-facing launch, creating potential rotor and turbulent conditions. Tomorrow.io shows gusts of 20–29 kts from the 060–070° quadrant in the afternoon, confirming a sustained NE push. The sounding's severe inversion caps the thermal boundary layer at 210–475m AGL — well below the ridge height needed for useful thermal climbs. Winds moderate slightly by 1600–1700 (11–12 kts from 030–040°) but Tomorrow.io gusts remain at 20–23 kts, keeping conditions punchy. No clean window exists today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 13–15 kts from NE with 80m winds at 19–25 kts — cross to behind on the W-facing launch. Strong shear and unfavorable direction. Not launchable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds persist at 13–15 kts NE with gusts to 19–29 kts per Tomorrow.io. BL reaches 375–435m but inversion caps any useful thermal development. Cross/behind flow continues.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Slight moderation to 11–13 kts from 030–040° by 1600, but Tomorrow.io gusts still 20–27 kts. Wind direction remains unfavorable for the W-facing launch. No improvement expected.

verdict.hazards: NE flow cross/behind W-facing launch all day; gusts 20–29 kts; strong low-level inversion caps thermals under 500m; significant wind shear

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Saturday April 4 looks dramatically calmer — GFS/HRRR show surface winds dropping to 0–5 kts with variable direction and overcast skies (100% cloud cover all day). The BL climbs impressively to 1680–1830m by afternoon, suggesting good thermal potential IF the cloud cover breaks. However, 100% overcast all day suppresses solar heating and makes thermal triggering unlikely. Winds are too light and skies too cloudy for a productive session. MARGINAL to NO FLY depending on cloud breaks.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Same overcast/calm pattern as 1750ft. Winds 0–5 kts, 100% clouds all day. Too calm and too cloudy for meaningful flying, though the light winds are at least benign. Training hill operations possible in light-wind practice if overcast lifts, but thermal flying is a no-go under solid cloud.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Tomorrow brings a complete wind shift — models show ESE to E winds of 8–10 kts at the surface in the morning, rotating to 110° (offshore) direction, which is completely wrong for Mussel Rock. 91–100% cloud cover all day. The afternoon sees a possible shift toward NW at 5–6 kts but under overcast skies. NO FLY for ridge soaring.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Similar to Ed Levin — dead calm (0.3 kts at noon!) under 100% overcast. BL reaches 1680–1830m but solar heating is blocked by cloud. Too light, too cloudy. NO FLY.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Sunday April 5 shows a much more promising pattern. Light variable winds morning, shifting to W/NW 5–10 kts by afternoon. BL climbs to 830–1540m by midday-afternoon with only 0–7% cloud cover — good solar heating and a proper thermal cycle expected. This could be the first genuinely flyable day of the weekend. LIKELY FLY, best window 12–4 PM.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Same improving pattern as 1750ft on Sunday. Light winds, clearing skies, good heating. A viable thermal day with 5–10 kts W/NW in the afternoon. Suitable for P2+ in afternoon thermal cycle.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Sunday shows very light winds (2–3 kts) in the morning from NE, shifting to W/SW by afternoon at 6–8 kts with 80m winds reaching 11 kts from 260–267°. This is approaching soarable territory for the afternoon. Some cloud (34–37%) by evening could limit heating but ridge lift from W flow is possible. MARGINAL, watch for afternoon sea breeze development.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Sunday looks good for Mission Peak — light morning winds, then W/NW 4–7 kts by afternoon with BL reaching 940–1540m and clear skies. A genuine thermal day. Best window 1200–1600. LIKELY FLY.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 (Monday April 6) data not yet available in the 4-day window at sufficient resolution. Trend from Sunday's improving pattern suggests continued benign conditions but monitor updated models.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Insufficient data beyond Sunday. Follow Sunday's improving trend but check Monday morning models for any gradient changes.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Insufficient data for Monday. If the W/NW pattern established Sunday continues, Mussel Rock could see usable afternoon sea breeze. Monitor.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Insufficient data for Monday. Sunday's good thermal pattern may persist into early week if the high pressure holds. Check updated models.

watchlist: NE wind event today (April 3) is the dominant story — persistent 13–15 kts from NE with gusts to 29–30 kts affects all inland sites (Ed Levin, Mission Peak) and makes them NO FLY
100% overcast forecast for Saturday April 4 at all sites suppresses thermal flying even though winds calm dramatically — confirm cloud cover forecasts with morning obs
Cold SST (12.3–12.5°C) at buoys 46012/46026 combined with 12-second swell period indicates a robust marine boundary layer that may delay coastal sea breeze development into next week
Tomorrow.io gusts at Ed Levin 600ft reach 30.5 kts at 1500 local — well beyond safe limits for any launch; do not be tempted by moderating mean winds
Sunday April 5 is the first potentially good flying day of the weekend — verify with Saturday evening model updates before committing to a trip
tldr: Today (Friday) is a NO FLY day across all Bay Area sites — a strong NE wind event is pushing 13–15 kts with gusts to 30 kts at the hill sites while Mussel Rock sits dead calm with wrong-direction flow. Saturday looks calm but overcast. Your best bet is Sunday, when clearing skies and a developing W/NW flow should bring proper thermal conditions to Ed Levin and Mission Peak — tentatively pencil in Sunday 12–4 PM.
